metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Verification command from `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_config_surface.py` to the actual path `tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py` (and update the golden file `tests/golden/config_surface.json` to include `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`).
evidence: Search for `test_config_surface` in tests/interfaces/contracts/ returns no matches.
tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py exists and is the real golden guard (confirmed by file read — it loads tests/golden/config_surface.json and checks _SECTIONS).
tests/interfaces/contracts/ directory listing shows no test_config_surface.py file.
Plan quote: 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_review_docs.py tests/interfaces/contracts/test_config_surface.py proves advanced documentation and config registration/golden coverage.'
findings.finding: The Verification section cites `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_config_surface.py` as a test to run for config registration/golden coverage, but this file does not exist. The actual config-surface golden guard lives at `tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py`.
scenarios: Running the Verification command as written will fail with 'file not found' for tests/interfaces/contracts/test_config_surface.py, giving a misleading CI failure unrelated to the actual implementation gap.
An implementer following the Verification section exactly will not run the real golden guard (tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py), potentially missing a broken config-surface registration.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_config_surface.py` with `tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py` in the Verification pytest command. Also add `verify.require_plan_review_for_close` to `tests/golden/config_surface.json` as part of this story's implementation.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to `AGENTS.md` (or a short ADR) capturing `pre_status_check` as the canonical pre-append hook pattern on `txn.transition_core`, including its ordering guarantees and error contract, so future callers can discover and reuse it without reading the plan-review feature doc.
evidence: 'Extend txn.transition_core(..., *, pre_status_check: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None] | None = None) as a generic local pre-append hook'
'txn.py imports no plan-review code'
docs/plan-review-gate.md is described as documenting the feature-scoped policy, not the generic hook contract
ABSENCE: no mention of AGENTS.md update or ADR for the new transaction hook pattern
findings.finding: The `pre_status_check` hook added to `txn.transition_core` is a new generic architectural contract on the core transaction primitive, but the plan only documents it inside the feature-scoped `docs/plan-review-gate.md`. No AGENTS.md update or ADR is planned to capture this as a reusable pattern for future callers.
findings.location: Implementation section — `txn.transition_core(..., *, pre_status_check: Callable[...] | None = None)` paragraph; `docs/plan-review-gate.md` AC #14
scenarios: A future engineer wants to add a second pre-append guard (e.g., a quota check) and doesn't know the `pre_status_check` pattern exists on `transition_core`.
A reviewer auditing the transaction layer finds a Callable parameter with no design-doc lineage outside a feature doc.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to `AGENTS.md` (under transaction/lifecycle patterns) like: 'Pre-append hooks: `transition_core` accepts an optional `pre_status_check` callback invoked under the write lock after state reduction but before STATUS append; raise `RebarError` to abort without write. See `docs/plan-review-gate.md` for the canonical example.' Alternatively, create `docs/adr/adr-NNN-pre-status-check-hook.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `pre_status_check` needs to be a fully generic `Callable` hook on `transition_core`, or whether a narrower, gate-specific mechanism (e.g. a dedicated `close_plan_review_check` parameter or an in-`close_ticket` wrapper that invokes the gate before calling the existing `transition_core`) would satisfy the locked-recheck requirement without a generic hook that has only one current caller.
evidence: grep for `transition_core(` in production code shows exactly ONE call site: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:478`
The plan states 'every existing caller defaults None' — confirming zero additional callers will use the hook at ship
Rule of Three: a generic Callable hook on the core transaction primitive without ≥3 current consumers is premature abstraction per A1
findings.finding: The `pre_status_check: Callable | None` parameter added to `transition_core` is a generic hook with exactly one current production call-site (`close_ticket` in `transition_close.py`); every other existing caller passes `None`. Rule of Three requires ≥3 existing call-sites before a generic abstraction is warranted. Although the locked-recheck requirement is real, a targeted parameter (e.g. `plan_review_pre_check: dict | None = None`) that is specific to this gate's locked-callback use would satisfy the same correctness requirement without premature genericization.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 6: 'Extend `txn.transition_core(..., *, pre_status_check: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None] | None = None)`'
scenarios: A future maintainer reads `transition_core`'s signature with a generic `pre_status_check` hook and assumes it is a general extension point, adding inappropriate logic for unrelated transitions.
At review time, the generic hook is a larger surface to audit for security properties (e.g. what can a caller inject into the critical section) than a narrowly-scoped gate-specific parameter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the filename of the config-surface golden artifact (e.g., `tests/interfaces/contracts/config_surface_golden.json`) directly in the implementation bullet so no filesystem search is required.
evidence: "update the config-surface golden" — no filename given
The test `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_config_surface.py` is named but the golden artifact it validates is not identified in the plan
findings.finding: The plan says to 'update the config-surface golden' without naming the golden file, leaving an executor to locate it by convention.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Register `require_plan_review_for_close: bool = False` on `_config_schema.py::VerifyConfig`, add the bool coercer to `_SECTIONS["verify"]`, and update the config-surface golden.'
scenarios: An executor unfamiliar with the codebase convention must search for the golden file before they can satisfy this step; if multiple golden files exist (e.g., per-section or per-format), the wrong one may be updated.
findings.suggested_fix: Append the filename: '…and update `tests/interfaces/contracts/config_surface_golden.json` (or equivalent).' If the codebase has a single canonical golden file, name it; if regenerated via a make target, name the target instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback statement to the Implementation or docs section: e.g., 'To revert enforcement after opt-in, set `require_plan_review_for_close=false` in config; existing close behavior is immediately restored with no state migration required.'
evidence: 'Default false preserves every existing close path' — acknowledges compatibility but does not describe the back-out procedure after opt-in.
T4 criterion: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
ABSENCE: No sentence in the plan states 'to revert enforcement, set require_plan_review_for_close back to false' or equivalent.
findings.finding: The plan does not explicitly state the rollback/back-out mechanism once `require_plan_review_for_close=true` is deployed and proves problematic. T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism, not just implicit flag reversal.
findings.location: Implementation section — `require_plan_review_for_close` config flag; also AC1
scenarios: An operator enables enforcement in production; close begins blocking tickets unexpectedly (e.g., due to a latent `unavailable` verdict); they need to know the exact rollback procedure under time pressure.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section (after the config-flag paragraph) add: 'Rollback: setting `require_plan_review_for_close` back to `false` immediately restores pre-enforcement close behavior with no state migration; no close events written under enforcement need to be reversed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence explaining why `close_plan_review_gate_check` lives in `gates.py` rather than `transition_close.py` — e.g., 'gates.py is the single seam for all opt-in verification gate checks so that any future non-transition_close close entry point automatically inherits enforcement without copying logic.'
evidence: Plan: 'Add _commands/gates.py::close_plan_review_gate_check(ticket_id, ticket_state, *, repo_root) -> dict as the sole close policy boundary.'

gates.py docstring: 'the plan-review start-work gate (plan_review_precheck) also lives here, once: starting work on a ticket goes through it whether via claim or a plain transition open -> in_progress'

transition_close.py owns all other close-specific logic (completion gate, force-close audit, signing, compact). Placing only the plan-review close gate in gates.py is asymmetric with completion gate placement in transition_close.py.
ABSENCE: No plan sentence explains why gates.py is the right home for this function rather than transition_close.py or a new _commands/plan_review_close.py.
findings.finding: The plan designates `_commands/gates.py::close_plan_review_gate_check` as 'the sole close policy boundary' but gives no positive rationale for WHY this function belongs in `gates.py` rather than inline in `transition_close.py` — the module that owns all other close-specific logic (the completion gate call, force-close audit, signing, compact-on-close). `gates.py` currently hosts the claim-gate (start-of-work) and the shared `gate_enabled` helper; co-locating the close gate there blurs the claim-vs-close boundary. The existing code makes the placement load-bearing: if another caller ever invokes close outside `transition_close` it needs to know which module owns the canonical enforcement seam, and the plan doesn't explain the reasoning.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 3: '_commands/gates.py::close_plan_review_gate_check as the sole close policy boundary'
scenarios: An implementer choosing where to place the function has no rationale to anchor the decision; a future engineer looking for close-gate logic has two places to check (gates.py and transition_close.py).
If a second close entry point ever emerges, the policy boundary location in gates.py is not self-evidently discoverable from the close path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation paragraph that introduces `close_plan_review_gate_check`, add: 'It lives in `gates.py` (alongside `plan_review_precheck` and `gate_enabled`) because that module is the single, discoverable enforcement seam for all opt-in verification gates — ensuring any future close entry point that imports `gates` inherits the check without duplicating policy logic in `transition_close.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the generic `pre_status_check` Callable hook on `txn.transition_core` vs simpler alternatives (e.g., performing both the precheck and a second locked re-read of `close_plan_review_gate_check` in `close_ticket` around the existing `txn.transition_core` call) that preserve the same TOCTOU guarantee without extending the transaction primitive's API surface.
evidence: Plan text: 'Extend `txn.transition_core(..., *, pre_status_check: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None] | None = None)` as a generic local pre-append hook; every existing caller defaults `None`.'

ABSENCE: The plan gives no comparison to alternatives — e.g., a pre-transition guard in `close_ticket` before the `txn.transition_core` call, a thin two-phase coordinator, or a separate locked-check function — that would also satisfy 'txn.py imports no plan-review code' without adding a generic hook to the transaction primitive.
Codebase: `txn.transition_core` (txn.py:184-314) is entirely linear with no existing callback or hook surface; adding one is a novel architectural extension to this primitive.

The plan's own text in paragraph 7 acknowledges a second check in `close_ticket` that passes a closure: this means close_ticket already holds the logic — the question is whether the hook on transition_core is warranted vs simply doing both checks in close_ticket around the txn call.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a generic `pre_status_check: Callable` hook on `txn.transition_core` — the load-bearing, write-locked, single-commit transaction primitive — without citing prior-art alternatives or justifying the novelty vs simpler established approaches. All existing close-gate logic (completion precheck, open-children guard) lives in `close_ticket` BEFORE calling `transition_core`, and a plain pre-call guard in `close_ticket` itself would satisfy the same TOCTOU requirement without adding a generic callback surface to the most security-critical primitive.
findings.location: Implementation, paragraph 6: 'Extend `txn.transition_core(..., *, pre_status_check: Callable[[Mapping[str, Any]], None] | None = None)`'
scenarios: A future developer adds a second pre_status_check consumer (e.g., for a different gate) without understanding the security implications of running arbitrary callbacks under the write lock.
The generic hook becomes a vector for accidentally importing plan-review or LLM code into the txn critical section through a callback closure that has wider imports.

findings.finding: The plan requires 'certified phase compatibility' as a blocking condition at close, but 'phase' is not an existing concept in the attestation system — there is no phase field in the manifest, no phase check in `compute_validity`, and no phase-aware signing in `sign_plan_review`. The plan does not specify what 'phase compatibility' means, how it is recorded at review time, or how it is checked at close time.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'It requires certified phase compatibility plus current own material'; AC line: 'A phase-incompatible attestation blocks close with review-plan remediation even when pin enforcement is disabled'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'call compute_validity(profile=close)' as if the `profile` parameter already exists on `compute_validity`, but the actual function signature at `attest.py:471` is `compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None)` — no `profile` parameter exists in the current codebase. The plan also says 'It requires certified phase compatibility' as part of the close profile's semantics, but no phase-compatibility logic exists anywhere in `compute_validity` or `attest.py`. Both the `profile` parameter and phase enforcement are deliverables of a sibling/predecessor story, not pre-existing behavior.
findings.location: Implementation §1 and §3: 'call compute_validity(profile=close) only when verify.require_plan_review_for_close is true' / 'It requires certified phase compatibility plus current own material'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit dependency statement in the Implementation: 'This story requires the predecessor story's delivery of the `profile` parameter on `compute_validity` and phase-compatibility enforcement in `compute_validity`'s plan-review branch before this story can be implemented. Do not wire `transition_close` until that predecessor closes.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies 'Repeat the same close-profile check under the write lock before STATUS append' but does not address the architectural tension: `transition_core` in `txn.py` owns the write lock and currently accepts no gate-enforcement parameters, while the plan-review gate lives in `gates.py`/`attest.py`. Threading a plan-review validity call into the locked critical section either requires adding a new gate-enforcement parameter to `transition_core` (changing the low-level txn module's interface) or acquiring the lock twice (breaking the single-critical-section guarantee). Neither approach is specified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Repeat the same close-profile check under the write lock before STATUS append'; AC: 'precheck ordering, and locked recheck races have interface-level tests'
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the existing pattern: pass a `plan_review_valid` callable (or pre-computed result token) into `transition_core` that is re-evaluated inside the lock before the STATUS write, similar to how `force_close_reason` is passed through today. This avoids double-locking and keeps the txn module's interface consistent.

findings.finding: The plan requires a 'recheck under the store write lock immediately before STATUS is appended,' but transition_core() in the existing codebase performs the locked STATUS write atomically as a single unit — there is no externally accessible pre-STATUS hook inside the lock. The plan does not specify whether transition_core gains a new callback/parameter, whether a new locking wrapper is introduced, or how the recheck result feeds into or aborts the atomic write. An executor cannot implement the locked recheck without this protocol.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Repeat the same close-profile check under the write lock before STATUS append.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'Pass a `plan_review_validity_fn: Callable[[dict], None] | None` callback to `transition_core`; when non-None it is called after the lock is acquired but before the STATUS event is appended, and may raise CommandError to abort the write without committing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified log/metric/trace emission for `unavailable` verdicts (and optionally other non-`valid` verdicts) in `close_plan_review_gate_check` so operators can detect systematic gate failures without relying on user reports.
evidence: 'Any unexpected local read/parser/signature exception is caught and returned fail-closed as verdict `unavailable`'
'Every failure uses the exact verdict-prefixed message and separate `review-plan` remediation; unexpected exceptions fail closed as `unavailable` without traceback'
No mention of logging, metrics, or tracing for new failure points in close_plan_review_gate_check
findings.finding: The plan specifies that unexpected exceptions are caught and returned as `unavailable` verdict without traceback, but no operator-facing instrumentation (log statement, metric, or trace) is specified for these catch-all failures. If a systematic issue (e.g., corrupted signature store, missing registry file) causes a wave of `unavailable` verdicts, operators have no visibility short of users reporting errors.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Any unexpected local read/parser/signature exception is caught and returned fail-closed as verdict `unavailable`' and AC: 'unexpected exceptions fail closed as `unavailable` without traceback'
scenarios: A corrupted or missing signature store causes every close attempt to return `unavailable`; operators see no errors in dashboards and cannot distinguish a systematic failure from expected behavior
A file permission regression silently degrades close enforcement for all users; no alerting fires
findings.suggested_fix: In `close_plan_review_gate_check`, after catching an unexpected exception and before returning the `unavailable` result, emit a structured log at WARNING or ERROR level (e.g., `logger.warning('plan_review_close_gate unavailable ticket=%s exc=%r', ticket_id, exc)`). Optionally increment a counter metric `plan_review_gate.unavailable_total`. Document this in `docs/plan-review-gate.md`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
